items.q: What is the difference between the motor effect and electromagnetic induction?
items.correct: The motor effect produces a force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field; induction produces an emf when flux linkage changes.
items.misconception: They are the same phenomenon described in different words.
items.random_wrong: The motor effect only works on DC; induction only works on AC.
items.why: Students see both involve coils, magnets and electricity and don't separate 'current causes force' (motor) from 'changing flux causes emf' (induction).

items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: What sets the pitch of a sound?
items.why: It's Frequency. “Amplitude” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Hertz. “Joules” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A guitar string vibrates 200 times per second. What is its frequency in hertz?
items.why: Exact answer: 200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
